coredata.dc:title: Monitoring zearalenone in corn flour utilizing novel self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence aptasensor based on NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Lijun
Ma, Shuai
Li, Libo
Liu, Xiaohong
Zhang, Jiayi
Li, Xia
Liu, Dong
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Accurate and early diagnosis of mycotoxin is particularly significant to the food and agricultural product safety. In the present work, a sensitive and effective monitoring method for zearalenone (ZEN) was exploited based on a novel self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor. The self-enhanced lumonophore was compounded by electrostatically combining amine-functionalized Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs) and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) together. Since the emitter and co-reactant simultaneously existed in the same nanoparticle, shortened electron-transfer distance and decreased energy loss was obtained. Therefore, self-enhanced ECL aptasensor based on the novel complex expressed the widest linear range of 10 fg mL−1–10 ng mL−1 and the lowest detection limit of 1 fg mL−1 for ZEN detection. More importantly, ZEN produced during the mildew process of corn flour was monitored by the developed aptasensor, which exhibited superior determination and potential application in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensor
Monitoring
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence
Zearalenone

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid otherkwds primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the EC and ECL behaviours of the modified electrode
The possible mechanism of self-enhanced luminophore
Construction and characterization of the ECL aptasensor
Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the ECL aptasensor
Monitoring the ZEN level during the mildew process of corn flour

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The preparation process of the NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore and (B) the fabrication process of the proposed ECL aptasensor.

TEM images of (A) NH2-Ru@SiO2 and (B) NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2. Inset of (A): TEM image of Ru@SiO2. (C) Zeta potential of different NPs. (D) Elemental distribution mapping of NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 of C, N, Si and Ru.

(A) CV and (B) Corresponding ECL-potential curves of (a) CHIT/NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE, (b) CHIT/NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE with NGQDs in PBS, (c) CHIT/NGQDs/NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE, (d) CHIT/NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE. Conditions: 100 mM PBS (pH 8.0), scan rate: 100 mV s−1, PMT: 700 V. (C) FL and ECL spectrum of NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2. (D) A schematic diagram of possible reaction mechanism.

(A) ECL characterization and (B) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the different modified electrode: (a) bare GCE, (b) CHIT/NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE, (c) Apt/CHIT/NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE, (d) ZEN/Apt/CHIT/NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE. ECL responses were measured in 100 mM PBS (pH 8.0); Scan rate: 100 mV s−1; PMT: 700 V. EIS were conducted in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The frequency range of EIS is at 100 mHz–1 MHz.

(A) Linear calibration between logarithmic value of ZEN concentration and the ECL intensity. Condition: scan rate: 100 mV s−1, PMT: 700 V. Inset of (A): ECL-Potential of various concentrations of ZEN: (a) 0, (b) 1 × 10−14, (c) 1 × 10−13, (d) 1 × 10−12, (e) 1 × 10−11, (f) 1 × 10−10, (g) 1 × 10−9, (h) 1 × 10−8 g mL−1. (B) Specificity of the proposed ECL aptasensor toward different interfering substances. (C) Repeatability for ZEN detection by 6 parallel measurements. (D) Stability of the proposed aptasensor in the presence of ZEN under consecutive cyclic scan of 13 cycles.
Monitoring ZEN produced during mildew process in corn flour. The abscissa represents the time, and the ordinate represents the detected content of ZEN. Inset: pictures of mildew in corn flour.

head.ce:title: Monitoring zearalenone in corn flour utilizing novel self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence aptasensor based on NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate and early diagnosis of mycotoxin is particularly significant to the food and agricultural product safety. In the present work, a sensitive and effective monitoring method for zearalenone (ZEN) was exploited based on a novel self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor. The self-enhanced lumonophore was compounded by electrostatically combining amine-functionalized Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs) and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) together. Since the emitter and co-reactant simultaneously existed in the same nanoparticle, shortened electron-transfer distance and decreased energy loss was obtained. Therefore, self-enhanced ECL aptasensor based on the novel complex expressed the widest linear range of 10 fg mL−1–10 ng mL−1 and the lowest detection limit of 1 fg mL−1 for ZEN detection. More importantly, ZEN produced during the mildew process of corn flour was monitored by the developed aptasensor, which exhibited superior determination and potential application in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Zearalenone (ZEN), universally known as F-2 toxin, is a nonsteroidal estrogenic mycotoxin produced mainly by Fusarium graminearum, and often exists in wheat, corn, sorghum, barley and soybeans (Chauhan et al., 2016, Wu et al., 2018). ZEN contaminations seriously influence the quality of agricultural product and the safety of food, further resulting in huge economic loss and significant threat to human health. Excessive ZEN in human body (10–100 μg kg−1) can lead to serious diseases, such as teratogenesis, carcinogenicity, neurotoxicity and abortion (Lin et al., 2016). Therefore, ZEN has been classified as the group III carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) (Zhang et al., 2017). And many countries, such as China (60 μg kg−1 for wheat and corn), France (50 μg kg−1 for cereal and cereal product), Australia (60 μg kg−1 for wheat) and Japan (1000 μg kg−1 for compound feeds), have established acceptable level of ZEN for different agricultural products (Wu et al., 2017; Kong et al., 2012; Zinedine et al., 2007).

In order to monitor the level of ZEN in agricultural product in succession, several analytical methods comprising gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Kinani et al., 2008), high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Soleimany et al., 2012a), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Kuzdraliński et al., 2013), surface-enhanced Raman scattering immunoassay (Liu, Hu et al., 2014) and fluorescence polarization immunoassay (Chun et al., 2009) have been employed. These techniques have achieved satisfactory results in practical application due to high sensitivity, good selectivity and excellent accuracy. However, the limitation of these methods cannot be deliberately ignored. For example, chromatographic methods require complex sample pretreatment process, expensive instruments and highly skilled technicians. By contrast, immunoassay-based methods provide rapid detection procedure, but they powerfully rely on the use of sensitive and costly antibodies. Therefore, it is urgently desirable to develop new methods for monitoring ZEN in agricultural commodities.

Recently, electrochemiluminescent (ECL) aptasensors have been developed as an emerging analytical method for determination of mycotoxin owing to their simple operation, low cost and easy of control (Gan et al., 2013; Lv et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015a). Among the ECL luminophores, Ru(bpy)3 2+ is the most widely employed emitter owing to its high luminous efficiency, good recyclability and excellent chemical stability (Xiong et al., 2015). In order to further enhance the ECL intensity and analytical sensitivity, Ru-complexes with self-enhanced ECL properties are proposed by scientists and synthesized widely (Wang et al., 2014, 2015b; Zhuo et al., 2014). In the self-enhanced ECL luminophore, Ru(bpy)3 2+ and its co-reactant are suitably designed and covalently fabricated in one composite so as to shorten the electron-transfer effectively and promote the luminous efficiency of Ru(bpy)3 2+ greatly (Wang et al., 2014). Up to now, many Ru(bpy)3 2+ based self-enhanced ECL complexes have been prepared by binding Ru(bpy)3 2+ with polyamidoamine (Wang et al., 2015b), polyethylenimine (Zhuo et al., 2014) and tris(3-aminopropyl)amine (Wang et al., 2016). However, these co-reactions belong to amine compounds, which have limitation of toxicity in real applications. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a novel alterative co-reactant addressing this issue. Recently, carbon nanodots or graphene quantum dots-based composites have been widely applied as new co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ due to their prominent water solubility, low toxicity and outstanding biocompatibility (Xu et al., 2013; Long et al., 2014; Carrara et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Qi et al., 2017). It has been reported that the carbon nanodots-based composites as co-reactants not only promote the luminous efficiency, but also improve the ECL performance. However, the research on utilizing nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots as co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ to fabricate self-enhanced luminophore and detect ZEN has not been reported up to now.

In the present work, a novel self-enhanced ECL aptasensor for ZEN determination and monitoring in corn flour was firstly developed. The self-enhanced luminophore was synthesized by electrostatically immobilizing nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) on amine-functionalized Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs) surface. The short electron-transfer distance and low energy loss between NGQDs and NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs produced excellent ECL efficiency. Based on the outstanding ECL properties, a novel self-enhanced ECL aptasensor for ZEN detection was fabricated, which exhibited the widest linear range and the lowest detection limit with excellent specificity and reproducibility. And specially, the developed method was successfully applied to monitor the ZEN level during the mildew process of corn flour.

Tris (2, 2-bipyridyl) dichlororuthenium (II) hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 0.5% chitosan and zearalenone (ZEN) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was bought from Aladdin. Triton X-100 was obtained from Dingguo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijng, China). Ammonium citrate tribasic, cyclohexane and 1-hexanol were got from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). ZEN aptamer (5′-GGA ATT CTT GAT GTT GCC TGG GAT TGT TTG GGC CTT GTG TTT TCT TCC GTT CCA ACT TAG TAG GAT CCC GAA-Amino-3′), supplied by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), was dissolved in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4, containing 20 mM Tris-HCl, 100 mM NaCl, 2 mM MgCl2 and 5 mM KCl, 1 mM CaCl2) to prepare the stock solution and stored in −20 °C for use. The detection phosphate buffered solution (0.1 M, PBS) was prepared by 0.1 M Na2HPO4 and 0.1 M NaH2PO4. All other reagents were analytical grade and used as received. The aqueous solutions throughout the experiments were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ).

Firstly, Ru@SiO2 NPs, which were further functionalized by APTES, were synthesized, as outlined in supporting information. Then, NGQDs were prepared in accordance with the previous literature (Yin et al., 2016). Finally, self-enhanced luminophore was prepared based on the electrostatic interaction between positively charged NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs and negatively charged NGQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A, 5 mg mL−1 NGQDs were mixed with NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs, and stirred for 12 h. Then, the obtained NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore was centrifuged and washed with water for at least three times.

The fabrication process of aptasensor was displayed in Fig. 1B. Prior to modification, glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm) was polished sequentially with 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm alumina power, followed by ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and ultrapure water thoroughly. After that, 6 μL NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore was dropped onto the GCE surface and dried naturally. Subsequently, 2 μL 0.5% chitosan solution (CHIT) was used to avoid the exfliation of NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 from the electrode. Then, ZEN aptamer was conjugated on the CHIT/NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE electrode surface through the electrostatic adherence between carboxyl of NGQDs and amino of aptamer at 4 °C for 12 h. After removing the unbound aptamer with buffer solution, the electrode was incubated with ZEN solution. Since the addition of aptamer and ZEN could block the electron-transfer, the quantitative detection of ZEN could be realized through recording the change of intensity based on the fabricated ECL aptasensor.

The standard addition method is utilized for ZEN detection in corn flour according to the previous method with little modifications (Liu, Hu et al., 2014). Firstly, different concentrations of stock solutions were put into 5 g corn flour, purchased from a local Auchan supermarket, to acquire corresponding samples spiked with different contents of ZEN (i.e., 90 pg mL−1, 900 pg mL−1, 9 ng mL−1, 90 ng mL−1). Then the spiked sample was maintained at room temperature for 24 h until methanol in the corn flour sample was evaporated.

ZEN extraction was carried out according to the reported method as follows (Wu et al., 2017): 1 g corn flour sample was mingled with 5 mL mixture of methanol and water (6:4, v/v), and vigorously agitated for 20 min. Then the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm min−1 for 10 min. Afterwards the supernatant solution was diluted 9 times with Tris-HCl buffer solution and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The morphology of different nanoparticles was characterized by TEM technology. Inset of Fig. 2 A shows that the obtained Ru@SiO2 NPs are monodisperse, spherical and uniform with the average diameter of 42.5 ± 3.0 nm. After functionalized with APTES, the diameter distribution of NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs is still narrow, but the average diameter slightly increases to 47.0 ± 3.0 nm (Fig. 2A). Meanwhile, the zeta potential results (Fig. 2C) indicate that after the Ru@SiO2 NPs are functionalized with APTES, its surface potential changes from −11.5 mV to 12.0 mV, indicating successful amination of the Ru@SiO2 NPs. Upon the introduction of NGQDs (corresponding characterization is shown in Fig. S1), self-enhanced ECL luminophore (NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2) is obtained with NGQDs evenly attached on the surface of the NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs by electrostatic interaction (Fig. 2B). Although the average diameter of self-enhanced ECL Ru-complex changes tinily (Inset of Fig. 2B), the electric charge (Fig. 2C) transfers from 12.0 mV to −25.1 mV, which should be attributed to the incorporation of negatively charged NGQDs (−31.0 mV). The change of potential also illustrates successful preparation of the self-enhanced ECL complex. The elemental distribution mapping spectra presents that C, N, Si and Ru elements are uniformly distributed in the NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore (Fig. 2D). Moreover, the functional groups on the surface of NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore were characterized by XPS spectra shown in Fig. S2. And successfully, all the XPS results demonstrate the novel self-enhanced NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore is synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the EC and ECL behaviours of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL superiority of the self-enhanced NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique. Firstly, CHIT/NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE was scanned in 100 mM PBS (pH 8.0) to serve as a reference. As shown in the CV curve (Fig. 3 A, a), NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs shows obvious oxidation peak and reduction peak respectively at 1.13 V and 1.01 V, resulting from the incorporation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecules into SiO2 NPs. Upon addition of NGQDs into the buffer solution (Fig. 3A and b), the oxidation peak current of Ru(bpy)3 2+ increases evidently and reduction peak current decreases, suggesting that NGQDs indeed possess an outstanding catalytic influence on the ECL system of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (Xu et al., 2013). For comparison, when the NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs and NGQDs are directly mixed and modified on GCE, it can be found that the oxidation peak is higher and the reduction peak is lower (Fig. 3A,c) than that of CHIT/ NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE, while the change degree is lower than that of curve b owing to the smaller quantity of NGQDs. Interestingly, upon the combination of NGQDs with NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs, the self-enhanced luminophore modified on GCE (Fig. 3A,d) exhibits maximum oxidation peak current and minimum reduction peak current, which may be benefited from the short electron-transfer distance and low energy loss in self-enhanced luminophore (Wang et al., 2016).

Corresponding ECL-potential curves were illustrated in Fig. 3B. The ECL intensity of NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs (curve a) is discovered to be ca. 550 a.u. at 1.13 V owing to the wrap of Ru(bpy)3 2+ into SiO2 NPs. With the addition of NGQDs into the buffer solution, the ECL intensity (ca. 5400 a.u.) is enhanced about 9.8-fold (Fig. 3B, b), indicating that NGQDs are able to be utilized as co-reactant of NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs definitely. When the mixture of NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs and NGQDs is modified on GCE (Fig. 3B,c), lower ECL intensity is obtained. However, after combining NGQDs with NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs into the same composite, the NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore itself can produce very strong ECL signal (Fig. 3B,d), 16-fold and 3-fold higher than NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs and the mixture of NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs and NGQDs, respectively. The results indicate that the NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore possesses excellent ECL properties, providing the potential basis for the construction of ECL aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism of self-enhanced luminophore
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL mechanism of the prepared self-enhanced luminophore was further investigated in detail. The FL and ECL spectra of NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore are shown in Fig. 3C. It can be apparently observed that the ECL spectrum coincides well with the FL spectrum of NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs, but away from that of NGQDs, indicating the ECL signal of the novel self-enhanced emitter is caused by NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs, and NGQDs act as co-reactant (Luo et al., 2017). Therefore, according to the previous reports (Xu et al., 2013; Long et al., 2014; Carrara et al., 2017) and the ECL spectroscopy results, the possible ECL mechanisms of NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 are proposed as follows (Fig. 3D): During the cycling, the NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore is firstly electrochemically oxidized and lose a proton to form NGQDs•–NH2-Ru(III)@SiO2 (Formula 1 and Formula 2). Then, the electron-transfer from NGQDs to Ru(III) by intramolecular reaction to produce an excited state of the complex (NGQDs-NH2-Ru*(II)@SiO2) (Formula 3), which emits light at 620 nm to produce an ECL signal (Formula 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of the ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent performance of the new self-enhanced luminophore, a novel ECL aptasensor for ZEN detection was constructed for the first time. As shown in Fig. 4 A, bare GCE (curve a) shows no ECL signal. When NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore is embellished on GCE, the ECL signal reaches up to ca. 8300 a.u. (Fig. 4A, b). After modification of ZEN aptamer, the ECL signal is quenched to 5400 a.u. (Fig. 4A, c). With the introduction of ZEN, a further decreased ECL response (ca. 50%) is received (Fig. 4A, d). The results demonstrate that the ECL aptasensing method developed for ZEN detection is feasible.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also performed to characterize the construction of aptasensor. Bare GCE displays a very small electron-transfer resistance (Ret) (120 Ω) due to the fast charge transfer kinetics (Fig. 4B,a). Upon the modification of self-enhanced NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore, Ret increases evidently (Fig. 4B,b) to 520 Ω. After immobilizing the aptamer (Fig. 4B, c) and ZEN (Fig. 4B, d), the value of Ret increases successively because both aptamer and ZEN have poor electrical conductivity. These results consist well with the ECL response, further confirming that the ECL aptasensor based on self-enhanced NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore for ZEN detection is successfully constructed.

Since circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy assay is an effective and sensitive method for studying the interaction patterns between aptamer and small molecules, the CD spectra were explored under buffer solution to further investigate the aptamer/ZEN reaction. As shown in Fig. S4, the CD spectrum of pure aptamer (black line) shows a positive band at 276 nm and a negative band at 245 nm, while that of pure ZEN (blue line) is around the baseline, almost zero signal. Then, the addition of ZEN in aptamer (red line) causes a slight decrease in the positive elliptic maximum and a little right shift of the negative minimum, which demonstrates that the structure of aptamer has been changed upon adding ZEN (Chen et al., 2013). This result further confirms the successful construction of ECL aptasensor.

To achieve good analytical performance, several experimental parameters, comprising the concentration of aptamer, reaction time between aptamer and NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2, binding time of ZEN with aptamer and pH value of the buffer solution were explored in detail.

Firstly, the influence of the aptamer concentration on the ECL signal of Apt/CHIT/NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2/GCE was investigated. Fig. S3A displays that the ECL intensity gradually decreases with the concentration of aptamers increasing, and when the aptamer concentration is 1 μM, the ECL signal tends to be stable. Therefore, 1 μM is selected as the optimal concentration. Then, influence of reaction time between aptamer and NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore shows ECL signal dramatically decreases from 2 to 10 h and then reaches a minimum value in 10 h (Fig. S3B). Hence, 10 h was designated chosen as the optimum reaction time.

Effect of the binding time of ZEN with aptamer is shown in Fig. S3C, and it presents the ECL signals with the binding time descend vividly from 20 to 60 min to the minimum value. After that, the ECL signal barely changes, so 60 min is selected for the proposed aptasensor. Finally, since the ECL system depends on the pH of buffer solution, influence of pH value on ECL intensity was inspected (Fig. S3D). With the increasing of pH value from 5.0 to 7.0, the ECL responses decrease remarkably and obtain the optimal outcome. Thus, the pH value is chosen as 7.0.

Under the optimum conditions, the analytical performance of the self-enhanced ECL aptasensor for ZEN detection was investigated. As can be seen from inset of Fig. 5 A, the ECL intensity of the aptasensor decreases with higher ZEN concentration. The ECL signal is inversely proportional to the consistence of ZEN in the range of 10 fg mL−1–10 ng mL−1 (Fig. 5A) with the detection limit of 1 fg mL−1. The linear regression equation is I = −1037.4–534.7 log CZEN with a correlation coefficient of 0.996. Compared with previously reported traditional methods (Table S1), such as high performance thin layer chromatography-fluorescence method (Ok et al., 2014), UPLC-MS (Soleimany et al., 2012b), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Liu, Hu et al., 2014), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Zhan et al., 2016), the developed ECL aptasensor not only has simple operation, but also presents high sensitivity with three orders of magnitude higher than that of the most sensitive method. By comparison with other biosensors, mainly including electrochemical biosensors based on DNA (Sadrabadi et al., 2016) and antibodies (Liu, chao et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2017), and fluorescence biosensor based on aptamer (Wu et al., 2017), the proposed ECL aptasensor expresses the widest linear range and the lowest detection limit. Finally, similar ECL biosensor based on antigen–antibody (Zheng et al., 2018) was compared with the present ECL aptasensor. It can be obviously seen from Table S1 that limits of detection of these two ECL biosensors are approximate, but fabrication process of the self-enhanced ECL aptasensor is more simple and rapid, suggesting its good application potential in real sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of the constructed aptasensor for ZEN detection, effect of different interfering mycotoxins on the detection of ZEN was investigated. As displayed in Fig. 5B, 100 ng mL−1 aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), fumonisin B1 (FB1) and ochratoxin A (OTA) cause minimal change of the ECL signal. However, 10 ng mL−1 ZEN leads to an evident decrease in the ECL intensity (58%). Besides, the mixture of ZEN and other three mycotoxins shows approximate ECL signal to pure ZEN, illustrating that the ECL aptasensor can analyze ZEN specifically.

Furthermore, reproducibility of the ECL aptasensor was evaluated by recording the ECL signal of six aptasensors fabricated parallelly (Fig. 5C). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the ECL intensity is calculated to be 1.89%. Besides, the fabrication reproducibility of the developed ECL aptasensor was also estimated. It still remained 96.2% of the initial response after storage at 4 °C for two weeks. In order to investigate the stability of the proposed aptasensor, seven different concentrations of ZEN were also studied in Fig. S5. The relative standard deviations (RSD) from low concentration to high concentration were 1.6%, 3.5%, 3.1%, 2.7%, 3.6%, 2.6%, 2.8% separately, demonstrating the good stability of our aptasensor. In addition, we inspected the stability of the fabricated aptasensor under consecutive potential scans for 13 cycles (Fig. 5D). The ECL signal shows slight change, further suggesting an excellent stability of the aptasensor. Therefore, the reproducibility and stability of the presented ECL aptasensor was receivable.

To verify the feasibility of the proposed ECL aptasensor in real sample, a standard adding method was used to determine ZEN in corn flour. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries are between 92.2% and 111.1%, and the average recovery of the four added levels was 100.2%. The accuracy, described by relative standard deviations (RSDs), was appraised utilizing three duplicates of the same added samples (2.07%–4.70%). Simultaneously, in order to confirm the reliability of the method, the recovery tests were also investigated by UPLC-MS. As can be seen in Table S2, the recovery results obtained by the ECL aptasensor are consistent well with that of UPLC-MS method, demonstrating that the present ECL aptasensor has good accuracy and reliability. More importantly, it can be easily found that the fabricated ECL aptasensor is able to detect ZEN reach to 9 × 10−11 g mL−1, whereas the UPLC-MS method can only discover ZEN of 9 × 10−8 mL−1. These consequences suggest that the sensitivity of the proposed aptasensor is three orders higher than that of UPLC-MS method, further indicating that the ECL aptasensor has great potential to sensitively detect and effectively monitor ZEN in corn samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the ZEN level during the mildew process of corn flour
ce:section.ce:para: Normally, the level of ZEN produced by moldy mildew in corn flour is invisible to the naked eye. Therefore, in order to find and solve problems as early as possible, early diagnosis of the moldy process is required. Based on the excellent performance of the ECL aptasensor, the content of ZEN produced during the mildew process of corn flour in a humid environment for 20 days was investigated (Fig. 6 ). From the inset of Fig. 6, the surface morphology of corn flour changes slightly from beginning to the third day, but the surface color of corn flour deepens gradually in the following three days. Interestingly, from the seventh day, some black spots start to appear on the surface of corn flour, demonstrating that the corn flour is becoming mold. After that, with the increase of incubation time, the degree of mildew in corn flour is getting more and more serious, and the corn flour almost becomes completely black until fifteenth day. Finally, when the corn flour is incubated for twenty days, the surface color of corn flour remains basically unchanged, indicating that the mildew may have reached saturation.

In order to understand the mildew process, the exact content of ZEN was monitored by the constructed ECL aptasensor. As can be seen from Fig. 6, after incubating for 24 h (1 day), the amount of ZEN is detected as 2.5 × 10−15 g mL−1, illustrating the early diagnosis time can be shortened to one day by the ECL aptasensor. Then the content of ZEN increases slightly from 2.5 × 10−15 g mL−1 to 1.2 × 10−14 g mL−1 during the next two days. However, the ZEN level jumps markedly to 2.4 × 10−8 g mL−1 from the third day to the tenth day, which may be related to the temperature and humidity environment. Subsequently, the level of ZEN tends to be stable with the prolongation of incubation time, probably due to the complete mildew of corn flour. The results agree well with that of naked eye observation. But more importantly, the diagnosis time of mildew process can be shortened from 6 days through naked eyes to 1 day using the ECL aptasensor, providing enough time to resolve the mildew problem.

In summary, a novel self-enhanced ECL aptasensor based on NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore was successfully fabricated for monitoring ZEN. Utilizing NGQDs as novel co-reactant of NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs could effectively promote the luminous efficiency. Further combining NGQDs with NH2-Ru@SiO2 NPs to synthesize a novel self-enhanced complex could shorten the electron-transfer distance and thus obtained higher ECL intensity. Therefore, the ECL aptasensor based on the self-enhanced NGQDs-NH2-Ru@SiO2 luminophore exhibited the highest sensitivity and the widest linear range for ZEN detection. Furthermore, when the aptasensor was applied for the analysis of ZEN in corn flour, good recoveries were obtained, which were consistent well with that of UPLC-MS method. More importantly, the fabricated ECL aptasensor showed three orders higher sensitivity than UPLC-MS. Finally, the ECL aptasensor was utilized to monitor the ZEN produced during the mildew process of corn flour, which could effectively reduce the diagnosis time of mildew process and provide a theoretical basis for solving the mildew problem.

This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675065 and 61801195), the “333 Project” (BRA2017390), the Six Categories Talent Peak of Jiangsu Province (NY-011), the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (2014-37) and Graduate Research Innovation Projects of Jiangsu Province (KYCX18_2264).
Conflict of interest
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
Submission declaration
The work described here has not been published previously and is not under consideration for publication elsewhere.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.04.050.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
